section.title: The Law and the Promise
section.sectionRationale: Paul explains how the law relates to the promise given to Abraham. The promise, made to Abraham and to his seed, who is Christ, came first and cannot be annulled by the law that arrived 430 years later. The law was a temporary guardian to lead us to Christ, until faith came. Now all who belong to Christ are one, neither Jew nor Greek, and heirs according to the promise.
section.thesis: The promise God made to Abraham and his seed, who is Christ, precedes and outranks the law, which was added temporarily as a guardian to expose sin and lead us to Christ; now that faith has come, all who are baptized into Christ are one, Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the promise to Abraham's seed, who is Christ, cannot be annulled by the later law; the purpose of the law as a temporary guardian until Christ; and the result, that all who belong to Christ are one and are heirs of the promise.

units.label: The promise outranks the law
units.note: to Abraham and to his seed, who is Christ

verses.text: Brothers, let me put this in human terms. Even a human {{diatheke|covenant}}, once it is ratified, cannot be canceled or amended.
The promises were spoken to Abraham and to his {{sperma|seed}}. The Scripture does not say, “and to seeds,” meaning many, but “and to your seed,” meaning One, who is Christ.
What I mean is this: The law that came 430 years later does not revoke the covenant previously established by God, so as to nullify the promise.
For if the inheritance depends on the law, then it no longer depends on a promise; but God freely granted it to Abraham through a promise.
Why then was the law given? It was added because of transgressions, until the arrival of the seed to whom the promise referred. It was administered through angels by a mediator.
A mediator is unnecessary, however, for only one party; but God is one.
Is the law, then, opposed to the promises of God? Certainly not! For if a law had been given that could impart life, then righteousness would certainly have come from the law.
But the Scripture pronounces all things confined by sin, so that by faith in Jesus Christ the promise might be given to those who believe.
Before this faith came, we were held in custody under the law, locked up until faith should be revealed.
So the law became our {{paidagogos|guardian}} to lead us to Christ, that we might be justified by faith.
Now that faith has come, we are no longer under a guardian.

units.label: One in Christ, heirs of the promise
units.note: neither Jew nor Greek, all one

verses.text: You are all sons of God through faith in Christ Jesus.
For all of you who were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ.
There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.
And if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's seed and {{kleronomos|heirs}} according to the promise.

diatheke.gloss: A covenant, a settled arrangement or will. Paul argues that even a ratified human covenant cannot be set aside or altered, so how much more the covenant of promise God made with Abraham. The word carries the sense of a binding arrangement established by God, which the later law could not annul or amend.

sperma.gloss: Seed, offspring. The word is collective in form but Paul presses that the promise to Abraham's seed points ultimately to one person, Christ, in whom the many are gathered. The single seed is Christ, and all who belong to Him become Abraham's seed and heirs, so the promise narrows to Christ and then widens to all who are His.

paidagogos.gloss: A guardian, the household servant who supervised and disciplined a child until adulthood. The law played this role, guarding and confining God's people, exposing sin, and leading them toward Christ. It was never the permanent way of salvation but a temporary custodian; now that faith has come, the child has grown and is no longer under the guardian.

kleronomos.gloss: An heir, one who receives an inheritance. Because believers belong to Christ, the true seed of Abraham, they become Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise. The inheritance is received by promise and faith, not earned by law keeping, and it belongs to all who are in Christ without distinction.

basic.context: Paul answers an obvious question: if salvation is by the promise to Abraham, why did God give the law at all? He shows that the promise came first and cannot be cancelled by the later law. The law had a real but temporary job: to expose sin and act as a guardian leading us to Christ. Now that Christ has come, all who trust Him are one, Abraham's heirs.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul thinks in terms of covenant history: promise first, law added later, Christ the goal. He reads the singular word seed as pointing to Christ. Read the passage as a map of how the law fits into God's plan without ever becoming the way of salvation.
basic.originalLanguages: A ratified diatheke, covenant, cannot be annulled, so the law cannot cancel the promise. The promise was to Abraham's sperma, seed, meaning one, who is Christ. The law was a paidagogos, a guardian, leading us to Christ. And all who belong to Christ are kleronomos, heirs, according to the promise.
basic.typology: Abraham's single seed is fulfilled in Christ, in whom the many become one. The law as guardian pictures the whole era before Christ, a childhood under supervision that gives way to the maturity of faith. Baptism into Christ and being clothed with Him fulfill the longing for a righteousness we could wear rather than earn.
basic.christ: Christ is the seed to whom the promise was made, the goal the law was pointing toward, and the one into whom believers are baptized and with whom they are clothed. In Him the distinctions that once divided people fall away, and all become one. He is the heir, and all who belong to Him share the inheritance.
basic.god: God is the promise maker who freely grants the inheritance to Abraham and keeps His word across the centuries. He is one, and His plan is one, moving from promise through law to Christ. He makes sons of God through faith and gathers a single family from every kind of person.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 12 and 15 record the promise to Abraham. Romans 4 shows the promise came by faith, not law. Colossians 3 says there is no Greek or Jew, for Christ is all and in all.
basic.oneStory: The promise to Abraham is the spine of the biblical story, and here Paul shows how it holds together with the law. The law served for a season, but the promise always aimed at Christ, the seed. In Him a united family of heirs, from every nation, receives what God pledged long ago.

context.literary: Paul argues from the lesser to the greater, using the example of a human will, then presses a single word of Scripture, seed, and answers an anticipated objection about the purpose of the law. The section builds to the ringing declaration of unity in verse 28 and the statement of heirship in verse 29 that ties chapter 3 back to Abraham.
context.historical: The agitators prized the law of Moses as the mark of belonging to God's people. Paul places the law in its historical setting: it came 430 years after the promise to Abraham, was mediated through angels and Moses, and served a temporary role. Baptism and being clothed with Christ mark the new basis of belonging.
context.canonical: The passage is central to biblical covenant theology, relating the Abrahamic promise, the Mosaic law, and their fulfillment in Christ. Verse 28 is a landmark statement of the unity of all believers, and verse 29 grounds the whole church in the promise to Abraham.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To explain that the promise to Abraham precedes and outranks the law, that the law had a temporary, preparatory purpose leading to Christ, and that all who belong to Christ are one and are heirs of the promise by faith.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the place of the law in God's unfolding plan and declares the present standing of believers. Its prescriptive force is that no one should return to the law as the way of salvation, since its guardian role has ended in Christ.

debates.question: What does it mean that the law was our guardian until Christ?

views.label: A temporary custodian that led to Christ
views.case: The paidagogos supervised and disciplined a child until maturity; the law confined and exposed sin, pointing forward to Christ, and its role ended when faith came.

views.label: A tutor that taught righteousness
views.case: Some emphasize the law's instructive role, but Paul stresses custody and confinement, not that the law could impart the righteousness it required.

debates.bearing: The law prepared for Christ but never justified; to return to it as the way of acceptance is to go back to childhood under a guardian after coming of age in Christ.

debates.question: How does neither Jew nor Greek, male nor female relate to remaining differences?

views.label: Equal standing and unity in Christ
views.case: The verse declares that all believers share the same status as sons and heirs; the distinctions that once divided access to God are abolished in Christ, without erasing all created differences.

views.label: A total flattening of all distinctions
views.case: Some press the verse to erase every role and difference, but the context is standing before God and membership in the one family, not the removal of every created distinction.

debates.bearing: The verse gloriously levels the ground at the cross, giving every believer the same standing as an heir, which is Paul's point against those who ranked Gentiles below Jews.

study.originalLanguages: Paul reasons from a diatheke, a covenant or ratified will, which even among humans cannot be annulled once settled, so the later law cannot cancel God's covenant of promise. He then presses the word sperma, seed, noting its singular form to point to one, Christ, in whom the many are gathered. He describes the law as a paidagogos, the household guardian who supervised a child until adulthood, a temporary custodian leading to Christ. And he ends with kleronomos, heirs: all who belong to Christ, the true seed, become Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise, receiving by faith what could never be earned by law.

typology.fulfillment: The single seed is fulfilled in Christ, in whom the many become one family of heirs from every nation.
The whole era under the law, a childhood under supervision, gives way to the maturity of faith now that Christ has come.
Fulfills the need for a righteousness we could wear, as believers put on Christ and are counted righteous in Him.

study.christ: Christ is the seed of Abraham to whom the promise was made, the destination the law was always pointing toward, and the one into whom believers are baptized and with whom they are clothed. In Him the old divisions of Jew and Greek, slave and free, male and female lose their power to rank people before God, and all become one. He is the true heir, and because believers belong to Him they share His inheritance, receiving the promise God made to Abraham long ago.
study.god: God is the faithful promise maker who freely granted the inheritance to Abraham and would not let the later law overturn His word. God is one, and His saving plan is one, moving from promise, through the guardian law, to fulfillment in Christ. He makes believers His sons through faith and gathers from every kind of person a single, united family of heirs. His covenant faithfulness across the centuries is the bedrock of the believer's hope.

crossReferences.note: The promise to Abraham and his offspring, the covenant the later law could not annul.
The promise comes by faith so that it may rest on grace, not by the law.
Having put on the new self, there is no Greek or Jew, for Christ is all and in all.
The law brings knowledge of sin and confines all under it, matching the guardian role.

study.oneStory: The promise to Abraham runs like a spine through the whole Bible, and here Paul shows how the law fits within it without ever replacing it. The law served for a season as a guardian, but the promise always aimed at Christ, the one seed. In Him a single family of heirs, drawn from every nation and every station, receives the inheritance God pledged so long ago, and the ancient promise reaches its glad fulfillment.

sermon.title: Promise and Law (Galatians 3:15 to 29)
sermon.why: Begg explains how the promise to Abraham relates to the law given later, why the law served as a temporary guardian leading to Christ, and how all who belong to Christ become one and heirs according to the promise.

quiz.q: Why can the law not cancel the promise to Abraham?

options.text: The promise came first and a ratified covenant cannot be annulled by a later law.
options.note: Verses 15 to 17 argue from the priority of the promise.

options.text: Because the law was never really from God.
options.note: The law was from God, but it does not revoke the earlier promise.

options.text: Because Abraham kept the law perfectly.
options.note: The promise rested on God's grace, not Abraham's law keeping.

options.text: Because the promise was only a suggestion.
options.note: It was a binding covenant God freely granted.

quiz.q: Who is the seed to whom the promises were made?

options.text: Christ, in whom all who believe are gathered.
options.note: Verse 16 identifies the one seed as Christ.

options.text: The whole nation of Israel only.
options.note: Paul narrows the seed to one, Christ, then widens it to all who are His.

options.text: Isaac alone.
options.note: Isaac was part of the line, but the promise points ultimately to Christ.

options.text: The angels who administered the law.
options.note: The seed is Christ, not the angels.

quiz.q: What was the purpose of the law?

options.text: A temporary guardian to expose sin and lead us to Christ.
options.note: Verses 19 to 24 describe the law's preparatory role.

options.text: The permanent way to earn righteousness.
options.note: The law could not impart life or justify; it pointed to Christ.

options.text: A means to divide people forever.
options.note: Its role was preparatory, ending in Christ who unites.

options.text: An accident that God later regretted.
options.note: God gave the law with real purpose, until Christ came.

quiz.q: What does verse 28 declare about believers in Christ?

options.text: There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female; all are one in Christ.
options.note: All share the same standing as sons and heirs.

options.text: Only Jewish believers are truly full members.
options.note: The verse abolishes that ranking; all are one in Christ.

options.text: Believers must erase every created difference.
options.note: The point is equal standing before God, not erasing all distinctions.

options.text: Social status still determines nearness to God.
options.note: All are one in Christ, regardless of status.

quiz.q: What is true of all who belong to Christ?

options.text: They are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise.
options.note: Verse 29 grounds every believer in the promise to Abraham.

options.text: They must still earn the inheritance by works.
options.note: The inheritance comes by promise and faith, not works.

options.text: They inherit only if they are physically Jewish.
options.note: Belonging to Christ, not physical descent, makes one an heir.

options.text: They receive nothing until the law is fully kept.
options.note: The inheritance is granted through the promise, received by faith.

questions: God kept His promise to Abraham across centuries and would not let the law overturn it. How does God's covenant faithfulness strengthen your trust in His promises to you?
The law served as a guardian to lead us to Christ. How has a sense of your own failure under God's standard driven you toward Christ rather than away from Him?
In Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female. Where do you still rank people, and how does your shared standing in Christ challenge that?
Believers are clothed with Christ. What does it mean for God to see you dressed in Christ's righteousness rather than your own record?
You are Abraham's heir according to the promise. How would living as a secure heir, rather than an anxious earner, change your week?

translationNotes.note: Seed renders sperma, collective in form; Paul presses its singular reference to point to one, Christ, in whom the many are gathered as Abraham's offspring.
Guardian translates paidagogos, the household servant who supervised a child to maturity; the term stresses custody and discipline, not classroom instruction.
Administered through angels by a mediator refers to the giving of the law at Sinai through Moses; the point is the law's mediated, secondary character next to the direct promise.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 3:15-29. No em dashes in this passage required substitution. Sermon verified this session from the Truth For Life series A Study in Galatians, Volume 1 by Alistair Begg.
